items.question: Who uses Gaussian Naive Bayes in practice?
items.answer: ML engineers and data scientists use GNB as a fast generative baseline on continuous tabular and sensor features — IoT fault detection, biometric screening, botanical measurements, and quick prototypes before heavier models.

items.question: What is Gaussian Naive Bayes?
items.answer: A generative classifier that picks the class maximizing log P(y=k) plus the sum of log Gaussian likelihoods log N(x_j; μ_{k,j}, σ²_{k,j}) per continuous feature j, under the naive conditional-independence factorization of P(x|y).

items.question: When should you choose GNB over logistic regression?
items.answer: When you have continuous features, small-to-medium labeled data, roughly unimodal per-class distributions, and want a fast closed-form generative baseline — especially if interpretable per-class means and variances matter.

items.question: Where do the μ and σ² parameters live in the model?
items.answer: For each class k and feature j, μ_{k,j} is the mean and σ²_{k,j} is the variance of feature j computed from training points with label k — stored as theta_ and var_ in sklearn GaussianNB.

items.question: Why multiply (or sum logs of) per-feature Gaussian likelihoods?
items.answer: The naive Bayes assumption treats features as conditionally independent given the class, so P(x|y) factorizes into a product of per-feature terms — see Conditional Independence Assumption for when that shortcut is justified.

items.question: How do you predict a class for a new vector x?
items.answer: For each class k, compute score_k = log P(y=k) + Σ_j log N(x_j; μ_{k,j}, σ²_{k,j}); output argmax_k score_k. In sklearn: fit GaussianNB, then predict or predict_log_proba on x.

items.question: Gaussian Naive Bayes vs logistic regression — modeling stance?
items.answer: GNB is generative: estimates P(x|y) with Gaussian per-feature likelihoods and applies Bayes. Logistic regression is discriminative: models P(y|x) with sigmoid directly. GNB needs distributional assumptions; logistic regression needs less generative structure but typically more data for comparable boundaries.

items.question: Gaussian Naive Bayes vs k-NN — memory and assumptions?
items.answer: GNB stores compact per-class μ and σ² (parametric, generative). k-NN stores all training points and votes locally (non-parametric, instance-based). GNB assumes Gaussian independent features; k-NN assumes similar neighbors share labels without a global density model.

items.question: Gaussian NB vs Multinomial NB — feature type?
items.answer: Gaussian NB models continuous real-valued features with Normal PDFs. Multinomial NB models discrete count data (word frequencies) with multinomial likelihoods. Pick the variant to match feature measurement type, not accuracy alone.

items.question: What if two features are highly correlated within each class?
items.answer: The naive product double-counts shared evidence — posteriors can be overconfident and accuracy drops. Mitigate with feature selection, PCA, or switch to discriminative models; see Conditional Independence Assumption for the underlying issue.

items.question: What if a feature has zero variance within a class in training?
items.answer: Without smoothing, σ²=0 makes the Gaussian PDF infinite at the training value and zero elsewhere — log scores become NaN or dominate incorrectly. Use variance smoothing (sklearn default) or add epsilon to σ².

items.question: Cause: Apply log transform to a right-skewed continuous feature before GNB. Effect?
items.answer: Per-class distributions become more symmetric and closer to Normal, improving the validity of Gaussian likelihoods and often raising accuracy and calibration versus fitting Normals on raw skewed values.

items.question: Cause: Increase training prior for a rare class via class_prior or resampling. Effect?
items.answer: Log-posterior scores add a larger log P(y=k) for that class, shifting decisions toward it — higher recall on the rare class at the cost of more false positives unless features strongly disagree.
